items.q: What is characteristic of Baroque music?
items.correct: Continuous bass line, ornamentation, terraced dynamics, and counterpoint.
items.why: Students project later styles backwards. Baroque (1600-1750) used basso continuo, harpsichord/organ, and terraced (sudden) dynamics. Piano hadn't yet replaced harpsichord.

items.q: What is a balanced antecedent-consequent phrase?
items.correct: Two phrases of equal length where the first sounds open (question) and the second resolves (answer).
items.misconception: Any two phrases of similar style.
items.random_wrong: A long melody with no breaks.
items.why: Students miss the FUNCTIONAL relationship. The antecedent typically ends on an imperfect cadence (open); the consequent answers with a perfect cadence (closed). Length matches; cadences contrast.

items.q: What does 'piano' (p) mean as a dynamic marking?
items.why: Students see 'piano' and think of the instrument. As dynamic, p = soft (from Italian). The instrument is actually a shortened 'pianoforte' (soft-loud) because it could play at varying dynamics, unlike the harpsichord.

items.q: In sonata form, in what key does the second subject normally appear in the exposition of a piece in a major key?
items.why: It's Dominant. “Tonic” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which composer is credited with significantly expanding the coda in sonata-form movements?
items.why: It's Beethoven. “Mozart” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many main sections does classical sonata form contain (exposition, development, recapitulation)?
items.why: Exact answer: 3 sections. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
